Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings, aiming for around 22 hours a week, but it rarely happened the way I planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd be really focused, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until panic set in. The sadness I've been dealing with made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling emotionally. When deadlines were close, I'd cram intensely, but during quieter weeks I'd often let things slide. With six online courses, it felt like there was always something competing for my attention. My extracurriculars also pulled me away sometimes, which made regular study times almost impossible. I tried to build habits, but they kept falling apart.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or realizing I'd fallen behind that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd see classmates posting in discussions and feel guilty enough to check what was happening. The LMS wasn't something I naturally gravitated toward—it felt more like an obligation than a tool. Occasionally I'd log in just to watch a video when I needed a break from reading dense material.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt automatic, especially early in the semester when I was more optimistic. I'd check it alongside my morning coffee, just scrolling through announcements. But that routine broke down when things got overwhelming with all six courses. After missing a few classes, the LMS started feeling like a reminder of how behind I was, which made me avoid it more.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I watched almost 290 minutes of video content because that format just clicked with my brain. The visual materials helped me understand concepts that reading alone couldn't convey. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when peers explained things differently than the instructor.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They felt dense and my eyes would just glaze over after a while. I'd download them intending to read later but rarely got around to it. Some of the optional practice quizzes I skipped because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams. Visual content that broke down complex ideas into digestible parts always felt valuable. I also appreciated anything that gave me quick feedback so I knew if I was on the right track. If something felt like busywork or repetitive, I'd lose interest fast. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me recognize when I was being too harsh on myself about what was 'productive.'
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford with everything else going on. Required work had this weight to it—I knew I had to do it or face consequences. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got bumped down the priority list. There was also this guilt with optional activities, like if I did them instead of required work, I was being irresponsible. It's a weird mental trap I'm still working through.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work with them on my tablet where I could annotate and draw connections visually. I have notebooks full of hand-drawn diagrams and concept maps that the LMS would never know about. Sometimes I'd use YouTube videos or other external resources to supplement what we covered in class. My actual learning was way more extensive than what showed up online.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I'd have my printed notes spread across my desk, working through problems by hand. I'd be deeply engaged, making connections, but the LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in. Group study with peers from my extracurriculars happened in person, not through the platform. Even when I was struggling with the material, I was often wrestling with it offline rather than clicking through online resources.
Q11: Yeah, this was basically my default approach. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then study from the downloaded version repeatedly. The LMS would show one view, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline. It was more convenient and didn't require internet, plus I could study on the bus or wherever. My engagement with the actual content was much deeper than my login patterns suggested.
Q12: The hardest times were when the sadness really hit and I couldn't motivate myself to do anything. There were weeks where getting out of bed felt like an achievement, let alone studying. My attendance dropped to around 34% partly because of this—some days I just couldn't face showing up. When multiple deadlines piled up across all six courses, I'd freeze instead of act. The CBT sessions helped, but the low points were still really difficult.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it represented all my failures and unfinished tasks. Confusion about course material made me procrastinate on engaging with it—I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later. My extracurriculars sometimes competed directly with study time, and honestly, they often won because they made me feel better emotionally. The stress of juggling everything made my LMS usage really inconsistent.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my mind was somewhere else, usually worrying about other things. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them, barely retaining the content. Sometimes I'd log in, click around aimlessly, and log out without accomplishing anything meaningful. Those sessions felt hollow even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. They required me to articulate my thoughts publicly, which felt vulnerable when I wasn't confident in my understanding. Assignments I found confusing got pushed back because starting them meant confronting what I didn't know. The avoidance made things worse, creating this cycle of stress and procrastination. My therapist and I have been working on breaking that pattern through the CBT sessions.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine driving my LMS usage. I'd have these intense bursts of activity in the days before something was due, then radio silence afterward. My assignment completion rate of 69% reflects how sometimes I'd miss deadlines despite the last-minute scrambles. The panic-driven approach wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived with six courses. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged with the LMS much at all.
Q17: When I got positive feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost to engage more. My assignment scores were actually pretty good—around 91%—which surprised me and made me feel capable. But when feedback was critical or I did poorly on exams, I'd withdraw and avoid the course for a while. The inconsistency between my assignment performance and exam scores frustrated me. Feedback definitely affected my emotional relationship with the course.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many components to track across six courses. Having clear modules helped somewhat, but the sheer volume made it hard to prioritize. I appreciated when things were organized visually with clear pathways. The structure didn't account for students like me who were struggling emotionally and needed more flexibility. A more forgiving structure with built-in catch-up periods would have helped.
Q19: Video content being readily available made engagement easier since that matched my learning style. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text-heavy documents. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't naturally fit how I wanted to learn. Timed elements or things requiring consistent attendance were difficult given my situation. More visual summaries and flexible deadlines would have made a real difference for me.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be engaging with materials, attempting to understand, but hitting walls when it comes to demonstrating that knowledge. Anxiety about submission could also play a role—fear of being judged. Sometimes high activity is actually unproductive clicking around while feeling lost. It might also be someone using the LMS socially rather than academically.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS engagement looked low because so much of my learning happened offline. Students who are efficient or have strong external resources might not need to use the LMS as much. It doesn't mean they're not engaged—they might just be engaged differently. The LMS only captures one narrow slice of learning behavior.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on videos, and whether you complete tasks. They're good at capturing frequency and timing of interactions. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They can't see offline study, emotional struggles, or the real reasons behind your behavior. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions in your head, making connections to other things you know. It's when you care about understanding, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement looks like creating visual maps of concepts and discussing ideas with friends. It's an internal state that external metrics can only approximate. True engagement survives even when motivation is low because curiosity keeps pulling you back.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with hand-drawn notes trying to make sense of difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture the emotional labor of showing up to study despite feeling sad and unmotivated. My peer interactions outside the platform, the study groups, the conversations—none of that appears in the data. The CBT sessions that helped me stay in the course aren't visible either. My resilience in pushing through despite everything is completely invisible to the system.
